elements.sent-2411-001: The problem of regularity and uniqueness are open for the supercritically dissipative surface quasi-geostrophic equations in certain classes.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this note we examine the extent to which small or large scales are necessarily active both for the temperature in a hypothetical blow-up scenario and for the error in hypothetical non-uniqueness scenarios, the latter understood within the class of Marchand's solutions.
elements.sent-2411-003: This extends prior work for the 3D Navier-Stokes equations.
elements.sent-2411-004: The extension is complicated by the fact that mild solution techniques are unavailable for supercritical SQG.
elements.sent-2411-005: This forces us to develop a new approach using energy methods and Littlewood-Paley theory.
elements.sent-2411-006: We define the $\al$-dissipative surface quasi-geostrophic equation to be \begin{equation}\label{eq.SQG}\tag{SQG$_\al$} \partial_t\theta + u\cdot \nabla \theta + (-\Delta)^{\alpha}\theta = 0; \quad u = (-\mathcal{R}_{x_2}\theta,\mathcal{R}_{x_1}\theta),\end{equation} where $\theta:\R\cross \R^2\to \R$ and $\mathcal{R _{x_n}$ is the Riesz transform with respect to the variable $x_n$ and $\al \in [0,1]$ is fixed.}
elements.sent-2411-007: In certain instances, these equations have physical relevance \cite{held1995surface,lapeyre2017surface .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The are more generally useful as a toy model for regularity questions for the Euler and Navier-Stokes equations.
elements.sent-2411-009: When $\alpha\in (1/2,1]$ the problem is sub-critical and global regularity is known.
elements.sent-2411-010: When $\alpha=1/2$ the problem is critical, in which case global regularity is also known, but is considerably harder to achieve.
elements.sent-2411-011: A selection of results addressing these cases are \cite{ConstantinCordobaGancedoWu,ConstantinMajdaTabak,ConstTarfVicolCMP,VariationsThemeCaffVass2009,KiselevNazarovVolbergInventiones2007,ConstWu,ConstVicolGAFA .}
elements.sent-2411-012: When $0<\alpha<1/2$ global regularity is, in general, an open problem.
elements.sent-2411-013: The same can be said of uniqueness of weak solutions, although certain classes of very weak solutions are known to exhibit non-uniqueness \cite{BSV , see also \cite{chengkwon2020non,novackquasigeostrophic .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this paper we consider $\alpha\in (0,1/2)$, which is the supercritical range for the dissipative problem.
elements.sent-2411-015: It is supercritical in the sense that quantities which have a maximum principle are not known to imply global regularity or well-posedness.
elements.sent-2411-016: Our present goal is to analyze sufficient conditions for regularity and uniqueness which are based on the relative sizes of small and large scales, interpreted through a Littlewood-Paley lens.
elements.sent-2411-017: This analysis is motivated by past work for the 3D Navier-Stokes equations by Albritton and Bradshaw \cite{AB2 and Bradshaw \cite{B1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The technical details are, however, substantially different.
elements.sent-2411-019: In particular, both \cite{AB2 and \cite{B1 primarily use mild solution estimates.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: These are known to break down in the supercritical SQG regime and we therefore need to develop alternative estimates based on energy methods.
elements.sent-2411-021: In addition to shedding new light on the dynamics of hypothetical SQG blow-up and non-uniqueness, the results of this paper demonstrate that the ideas in \cite{AB2,B1 can be adapted to PDEs with different qualitative properties compared to the 3D Navier-Stokes equations.}
elements.sent-2411-022: SQG has a natural scaling: If $\th$ is a solution to \eqref{Eq.SQG , then so is $\th_\la := \la^{2\al-1}\th(\la x,\la^{2\al t)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: We therefore see that the Sobolev space $\dot H^{2-2\al}$ is a scaling invariant space.
elements.sent-2411-024: We will refer to such spaces as critical.
elements.sent-2411-025: Roughly speaking, local well-posedness is known in $\dot H^s$ for $s>2-2\al$ with partial results if $s=2-2\al$, see \cite{Vincent for a comprehensive review of results in this direction, while global existence of weak solutions, in the sense of Marchand, is known for $-4/3<s<2-2\al$ \cite{marchand .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Our first regularity result should be viewed through the lens of subcritical local well-posedness theories.
elements.sent-2411-027: Let us recall Ju's local well-posedness theorem in Sobolev spaces \cite{Ju : If $s>2-\alpha$ where $\alpha\in (0,1/2)$ and $\theta_0\in \dot H^{s}\cap L^2$, then there exists $T=T(\|u_0\|_{\dot H^s )>0$ and a unique strong solution $\theta$ to \eqref{Eq.SQG so that \begin{equation}\th\in L^\I(0,T;H^s )\cap L^2(0,T;H^{s+\al}),\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\| \th (t)\|_{\dot H^{s}} \leq 2\| \th_0\|_{\dot H^{s}},\end{equation} provided $t\in (0,T_{min )$ where \begin{equation}T_{min} \| \th_0\|_{\dot H^s}^{\frac {2\al} {s-2+2\al}} = C_0(s,\al),\end{equation} and $C_0(s,\al)$ is a universal constant depending on $s$ and $\al$ and $T_{min < T$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: To confirm this in \cite{Ju , refer to the inequality below \cite[(3.5)]{Ju .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: We will view $T_{min}$ as the minimum guaranteed time of existence.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our first regularity result says that, if $\|\th_0\|_{\dot H^s}$ is held fixed but activity is concentrated on high modes, then Ju's existence time can be extended.
elements.sent-2411-032: Then, there exists a constant $C=C(s,\al)$ so that, for $J=J(t)$ satisfying \begin{equation}2^{2\al J} \geq C \|\th_0\|_{\dot H^s}^{1 + \frac {4\al} {s-2+2\al}} T_*^{1+\frac {s-2+s\al}{2\al}},\end{equation} if \begin{equation}\label{freqSp} \frac {\| \Delta_{<J} \th_0 \|_{\dot H^s}} {\| \Delta_{\geq J} \th_0\|_{\dot H^s}} \leq \frac \ga {4 C_b},\end{equation} where $C_b$ is a universal constant and \begin{equation}\ga = \bigg( \frac {T_{min}} {T_*} \bigg)^{\frac {s-2+2\al} {2\al}} ,\end{equation} then $\th$ can be continued smoothly up to time $T_*$ and satisfies \begin{equation}\sup_{0<t<T_*}\| \th (t)\|_{\dot H^s}\leq 2 \| \th_0\|_{\dot H^s}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: The idea behind Theorem \ref{thrm.reg1 is that a solution $\th$ of \eqref{Eq.SQG can be viewed as a linear part---this would solve the fractional heat equation---and a nonlinear part which appears with a time integral as in Duhamel's principle.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The fractional heat part wants to dissipate activity at small scales.
elements.sent-2411-035: So, if the bulk of the activity is concentrated at small scales, then the linear part of the solution will decay rapidly.
elements.sent-2411-036: The nonlinear part starts at zero and grows at an algebraic rate.
elements.sent-2411-037: Taken together and assuming activity is concentrated at high enough frequencies, these facts suggest that we can find a time at which the $\dot H^s$ norm has become as small as we want to guarantee that, upon re-solving, the solution extends past $T_*$.
elements.sent-2411-038: This idea is visible in Proposition \ref{PROP , which provides the heavy lifting for our regularity results.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Let us note that while this idea is motivated by work for 3D Navier-Stokes \cite{AB2 , its implementation here is quite different due to the fact that mild solution estimates aren't useful in the supercritical SQG range---indeed the preceding intuitive picture is slightly misleading as the Duhamel part cannot be controlled directly.}
elements.sent-2411-040: This necessitated re-imagining the argument from \cite{AB2 in terms of energy inequalities, which is the main technical contribution of this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In \cite{AB2 , the solutions were mild so their linear and non-linear parts are built into the solution formula.}
elements.sent-2411-042: It is less clear when using energy estimates how to exploit the fact that small scales are eliminated rapidly by diffusion.
elements.sent-2411-043: We are able to identify the correct mechanism by considering differential inequalities for the energy associated with individual Littlewood-Paley modes---see the proof of Proposition \ref{PROP .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Our next theorem considers a Type 1 blow-up scenario and establishes an upper bound on the rate at which small scales can activate.
elements.sent-2411-045: Let $\th :\R^2\times (0,T_{max ) \to \R$ belong to $L^\I_{loc ((0,T_{max ); \dot H^{s )\cap L^\I(0,T_{max ;L^2)$ be a unique strong solution on $\R^2\times (0,T_{max )$ which is singular at $T_{max}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Assume further that there exists $C_*$ so that \begin{equation}\bigg( \frac {C_0(s,\al)} {T_{max}-t} \bigg)^{(s-2+2\al) / (2\al)} \leq \|\th(t)\|_{\dot H^s}\leq \bigg( \frac {C_*} {T_{max}-t} \bigg)^{(s-2+2\al) / (2\al)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: Then, there exists $J$ with $2^{2\al J}\sim (T_{max -t)^{-1}$ and a constant $c_*>0$ so that \begin{equation}\inf_{0<t<T_{max}} \frac {\| \Delta_{<J} \th (t)\|_{\dot H^s}} {\| \Delta_{\geq J} \th (t)\|_{\dot H^s}} >c_*.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The lower bound stated in the theorem does not need to built into the assumptions as it is a necessary condition for blow-up.
elements.sent-2411-049: This theorem reveals that low modes (as identified by a time dependent threshold) must be active to some extent as a hypothetical singularity develops.
elements.sent-2411-050: This is somewhat counterintuitive as high modes are known to drive singularity formation.
elements.sent-2411-051: In essence, the Type 1 condition restricts the rate at which activity can pass to very small scales, indicating complimentary scales must be active.
elements.sent-2411-052: There is a vast literature on conditional regularity criteria for the supercritical SQG equations, some of which is reviewed before Theorem \ref{thrm.reg3 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: We feel it is presently appropriate to only mention thematically similar papers of Dai \cite{dai (see also \cite{CS2 ) and Wu \cite{Wu which explore the role of high frequencies on one hand and sparse sets in the Fourier variable on the other.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: One motivation for Albritton and Bradshaw's work on Navier-Stokes \cite{AB2 , on which the present note is based, concerns a regularity program of Gruji\'c and coauthors \cite{Gru,BrFaGr,GrXu1,GrXu2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: We believe this program can be re-formulated through the lens of ``frequency sparseness,'' which amounts to conditions like \eqref{freqSp .}
elements.sent-2411-056: This approach avoids the use of analyticity coupled with $L^\I$ estimates.
elements.sent-2411-057: For the SQG problem, this avoidance seems necessary because $L^\I$ is not a sub-critical space and, consequently, the analyticity argument in \cite{Gru , which involve the maximum modulus principle, cannot be applied directly.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Some of the later results of Gruji\'c and Xu \cite{GrXu2 relate to the generalized Navier-Stokes with dissipation below the Lions exponent $(-\Delta)^{5/4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: We believe that the present formulation of ``frequency sparseness'' could allow these results to be adapted to the supercritical SQG equations and are curious to explore this in future work.
elements.sent-2411-060: We conclude our discussion of regularity with a new proof of an endpoint regularity criteria.
elements.sent-2411-061: This should be compared to \cite[Theorem 3.4]{Dong1 which involves regularity criteria where the time-integral is $L^{r_0}$ and $r_0<\I$; see also \cite{ConstWu .}}
elements.sent-2411-062: When $r_0=\I$, there is a corresponding statement due to Chae and Lee in $B^{2-2\al _{2,1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Chae also formulated similar results in terms of $\nabla^\perp \theta$ \cite{Chae , see also Yuan's paper \cite{Yuan .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: An endpoint case is considered by Dong and Pavlovi\'c \cite{Dong2 (see also \cite{CS1 ) assuming $\th \in C (0,T; \dot B^{1-2\al _{\I,\I )$, which is close to a result involving the time $L^\I$-norm and, in light of the version of their result which allows small jumps, can be used to prove a smallness-implies-regularity version.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Because $\dot B^{2-2\al _{2,\I}$ embeds continuously in $\dot B^{1-2\al _{\I,\I}$, the result of Dong and Pavlovi\'c encompasses some of what follows.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: A novel aspect of our result compared to these others is that only scales larger than a function of $\| \th \|_{\dot H^s (t)$ appear in the regularity criteria---a similar theme appears in \cite{BG for the Navier-Stokes equations.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Based on this difference, as well as the fact that the approach of the proof is new compared to \cite{Dong2 , we believe it is worth including.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Assume $\th\in C((0,T_*);\dot H^s )$ is a strong solution to \eqref{Eq.SQG with data $\th_0$ and $T_*$ is the first possible blow-up time.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Then there exists a small constant $\epsilon_*>0$ and function $J(t)$ with \begin{equation}2^{(2-s+2\al)J(t)} = C(s,\al) \|\th(t)\|_{\dot H^s},\end{equation} so that if \begin{equation}\sup_{0<t<T^*}\|\Delta_{<J(t)} \theta(x,t)\|_{\dot B^{2-2\alpha}_{2,\infty}}\leq \epsilon_*,\end{equation} then $T^*$ is not a blow-up time and the solution can be smoothly extended past $T_*$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Non-uniqueness can, in theory, ruin the predictive power of simulations.
elements.sent-2411-072: It is therefore important to understand the severity of a hypothetical non-uniqueness scenario, which can be analyzed by providing growth rates for the error, which is the difference between two solutions, or establishing necessary properties of the error.
elements.sent-2411-073: The latter could be obtained by formulating uniqueness criteria in terms of the error.
elements.sent-2411-074: It is not obvious what such uniqueness criteria would look like.
elements.sent-2411-075: Some work in this direction has been carried out for the 3D Navier-Stokes equations by the second author \cite{B1 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Presently, the most likely candidates for non-uniqueness for 3D Navier-Stokes within a physical class are based on self-similar solutions \cite{GuSv,JS1,JS2,AbC .}
elements.sent-2411-077: The scaling of these solutions implies certain relationships between the scales of the error.
elements.sent-2411-078: Denoting by $w$ the difference of two self-similar solutions sharing the self-similar initial data $u_0$, we have, for instance, that individual Littlewood-Paley modes vanish as $t\to 0^+$, in particular, \begin{equation}\| \Delta_{< J} w\|_{L^\infty}(t)\lesssim_{{u_0}} 2^{4J} t^{3/2},\end{equation} provided the two solutions are in a reasonable class \cite{B1 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: On the other hand, due to the exact scaling of the error, \begin{equation}\| w \|_{L^\infty}(t)= t^{-1/2}\|w\|_{L^\infty}(1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-080: So, as $t\to 0^+$, $\| w_{\geq J}\|_{L^\infty}$ is blowing up while $\|w_{<J}\|_{L^\infty}\to 0$, indicating activity is concentrating on smaller and smaller scales.
elements.sent-2411-081: In other words, in self-similar non-uniqueness for the Navier-Stokes equations the activity in the error would start at infinitesimally small scales and propagate to larger scales as time passes.
elements.sent-2411-082: One goal of \cite{B1 is to extend this to general classes which do not have an exact scaling property.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Presently, we pursue this goal for the supercritical surface quasi-geostrophic equations.
elements.sent-2411-084: Note that hypothetical self-similar non-uniqueness has not been thoroughly explored for SQG, although the existence of large self-similar solutions has been developed in the critical case by Albritton and the second author as a preliminary step in this direction \cite{AB2 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Such solutions have not been constructed in the supercritical case.
elements.sent-2411-086: We presently adapt some of the ideas of \cite{B1 to supercritical SQG.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Compared to \cite{B1 , it is again necessary to formulate proofs in terms of energy methods.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We have in mind scenarios where a rough initial data possibly generates multiple solutions which are regular at $t>0$.
elements.sent-2411-089: This is the case for the hypothetical self-similar solutions to the Navier-Stokes equations on which our analysis is inspired.
elements.sent-2411-090: For this reason, our solutions are viewed as possibly non-unique but are also strong solutions for $t>0$---this manifests, e.g., as the assumption that $\|\nabla \theta\|_\I(t)\in L^\I_{loc ( (0,T))$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: We stress that they do not belong to a known uniqueness class on the full interval $[0,T]$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Our first theorem asserts that high modes in the error cannot be subordinate to low modes as $t\to 0^+$, assuming the error is non-zero.

elements.sent-2411-141: Now taking the inner product with $\theta_j$ and using Bernstein's inequality (which involves the constant $\la$ appearing below) we get that \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dt}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2}^2 + \lambda 2^{2\alpha j}\|\theta_j\|^2_{L^2} + (u\cdot \nabla\Delta_j \theta,\theta_j) = ([u,\Delta_j]\nabla\theta,\theta_j).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-142: From this we infer \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dt}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2}^2 + \lambda 2^{2\alpha j}\|\theta_j\|^2_{L^2} \leq \|[u,\Delta_j]\nabla\theta\|_{L^2}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2},\end{equation} and upon dividing through by $\|\theta_j\|_{L^2}$, \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dt}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2} + \lambda 2^{2\alpha j}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2} \leq \|[u,\Delta_j]\nabla\theta\|_{L^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-143: We now apply the commutator estimate \eqref{ineq.commutator to obtain \begin{equation}\begin{split} \frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dt}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2} + \lambda 2^{2\alpha j}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2} \leq \|[u,\Delta_j]\nabla\theta\|_{L^2} \leq Cc_j2^{-(2s-2)j}\|u\|_{\dot{H}^{s}}\|\nabla \theta\|_{\dot{H}^{s-1}} \leq Cc_j2^{-(2s-2)j}\|\theta\|_{\dot{H}^{s}}^2. \end{split}\end{equation} Observe that \begin{equation}\frac d {dt} (e^{\la 2^{2\al j}t }\|\theta_j\|_{L^2}(t) ) = e^{\la 2^{2\al j}t } (\frac{d}{dt}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2} + 2\lambda 2^{2\alpha j}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2} ).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Integrating in time we therefore obtain \begin{equation}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2}(t) \leq e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\|\theta_j(0)\|_{L^2} + Cc_j2^{-(2s-2)j}\int_0^te^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda (t-s)}\|\theta(s)\|_{\dot H^{s}}^2 \, ds.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-036: \begin{equation}\|\theta_j\|_{L^2}(t) \leq e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\|\theta_j(0)\|_{L^2} + Cc_j2^{-(2s-2)j}\int_0^te^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda (t-s)}\|\theta(s)\|_{\dot H^{s}}^2 \, ds.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-145: Now multiplying by $2^{sj}$ and applying the $l^2(\Z)$-norm with respect to $j$, we get that \begin{equation}\|\theta(t)\|_{\dot H^{s}} \leq I + II,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}I = \left(\sum_{j\in \Z}\left(2^{sj}e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\la t}\|\theta_j(0)\|_{L^2}\right)^2\right)^{1/2},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}II = \left(\sum_{j\in \Z}\left( Cc_j2^{-sj +2j}\int_0^te^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda (t-s)}\|\theta(s)\|_{\dot H^{s}}^2 \, ds\right)^2\right)^{1/2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-147: We will do this for an arbitrary time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-148: We will then give a condition on $t$ when we bound $II$.
elements.sent-2411-149: We start by writing \begin{equation}I \leq I_a+I_b,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}I_a = \left(\sum_{j\geq J}\left(2^{sj}e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\|\theta_j(0)\|_{L^2}\right)^2\right)^{1/2} \text{ and } I_b = \left(\sum_{j<J}\left(2^{sj}e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\|\theta_j(0)\|_{L^2}\right)^2\right)^{1/2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-150: We evaluate $I_a$ as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \left(\sum_{j\geq J}\left(2^{sj}e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\|\theta_j(0)\|_{L^2}\right)^2\right)^{1/2} \leq \|\theta_{\geq J}(0)\|_{\dot H^{s}}\left(\sum_{j\geq J}\left(e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\right)^2\right)^{1/2} \leq \frac{C}{2^{2\alpha J + 1}t}\|\theta_{\geq J}(0)\|_{\dot H^{s}}. \end{split}\end{equation} We may choose $J=J(t)$ according to $2^{2\alpha J}\geq 2C/(\ga t)$ so that \begin{equation}\label{ineq.Ia} I_a\leq \frac{\gamma}{4}\|\theta(0)\|_{\dot H^{s}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-042: \begin{equation} \left(\sum_{j\geq J}\left(2^{sj}e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\|\theta_j(0)\|_{L^2}\right)^2\right)^{1/2} \leq \|\theta_{\geq J}(0)\|_{\dot H^{s}}\left(\sum_{j\geq J}\left(e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\right)^2\right)^{1/2} \leq \frac{C}{2^{2\alpha J + 1}t}\|\theta_{\geq J}(0)\|_{\dot H^{s}}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-151: For the $I_b$, since $e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t \leq 1$ for all $j$ and $t$, we simply have \begin{equation}\label{ineq.Ib} \begin{split} I_b \leq \left(\sum_{j<J}\left(2^{sj}e^{-2^{2\alpha j}\lambda t}\|\theta_j(0)\|_{L^2}\right)^2\right)^{1/2} \leq C_b\|\Delta_{<J}\theta_0\|_{\dot H^{s}} \leq \frac \ga 4 \|\Delta_{\geq J}\th_0 \|_{\dot H^{s}} , \end{split}\end{equation} where we have used the assumption \eqref{assumption.freqSparse .}}
